scope: The designed end of the audio mini-spectrum.
key_concepts: Audio data naturally exists as a 1D raw waveform (amplitude over time), which is dense and difficult for classical models to process directly.
To extract meaningful patterns, engineers transform audio into the frequency domain, creating representasi yang dirancang manusia (handcrafted features).

Spectrogram: a 2D visual representation of audio (time on X-axis, frequency on Y-axis, intensity as color). Treating spectrograms as 'images' allows the reuse of computer vision techniques for audio classification.
Mel-Frequency Cepstral Coefficients (MFCC): heavily engineered features designed specifically to mimic the non-linear perception of the human ear. It is the gold standard for speech recognition.
Chroma features: capturing the energy distribution across the 12 musical pitch classes, primarily used for music genre classification and chord recognition.

definitions.definition: The raw, 1D digital representation of an audio signal, recording changes in air pressure amplitude over time.
A visual representation of the spectrum of frequencies of a signal as it varies with time.
A representation of the short-term power spectrum of a sound, based on a linear cosine transform of a log power spectrum on a nonlinear mel scale.

examples: Explain how converting a voice recording into a spectrogram turns a confusing 1D squiggly line into a 2D 'picture' of sound, where vowels and consonants look like distinct shapes.

relations.builds_toward: ch12_s06 (how modern models bypass these designed features and learn directly from the raw waveform)

exclusions: Do not explain the Fourier Transform (FFT) mathematics in depth.
Do not discuss deep learning models for audio yet.
